{"id":"ss-0dh.6","title":"Shared infrastructure: Skill discovery & frontmatter parsing library","description":"## Overview
Multiple planned commands (budget, verify, lint, policy, test) need to discover installed skills and parse their SKILL.md frontmatter. The audit command already does this, but the logic is embedded in the audit orchestrator. Extract shared skill discovery and frontmatter parsing into reusable modules that all commands can consume.

## Current State
- `audit/index.ts` discovers skill files and parses frontmatter
- `audit/extractors/` extract packages, commands, URLs from skill content
- This logic is coupled to the audit pipeline

## What to Extract
1. **Skill Discovery** — Find all SKILL.md files in a directory (recursive), respecting .gitignore
2. **Frontmatter Parsing** — Parse YAML frontmatter from SKILL.md files into typed objects
3. **Section Parsing** — Split a skill into sections by markdown headings (needed by budget for per-section token counts, by verify for structural diff)
4. **Content Extraction** — The extractors (packages, commands, URLs) should be importable by any command

## Design Principle
From CLAUDE.md: 'This extractor/checker/reporter pattern is the template for all future commands.' The shared infrastructure enables this by making extractors reusable.

## Proposed Location
```
src/
  shared/
    discovery.ts      # Find SKILL.md files
    frontmatter.ts    # Parse YAML frontmatter
    sections.ts       # Parse markdown into sections
    types.ts          # Shared types (SkillFile, Frontmatter, Section)
```

Or refactor audit/extractors to be importable from a common path.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Skill discovery works independently of audit command
- [ ] Frontmatter parsing returns typed objects
- [ ] Section parsing handles nested headings
- [ ] Audit command still works after refactor (no behavior change)
- [ ] All extractors importable from outside audit/
- [ ] Unit tests for discovery, parsing, and section splitting","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","owner":"voodootikigod@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-03T18:46:58Z","created_by":"Chris Williams","updated_at":"2026-03-03T18:46:58Z"}

{"id":"ss-0dh.1","title":"Implement `skillsafe budget` command — Context Budget Analysis","description":"## Overview
Implement the `skillsafe budget` command that measures and reports the token cost of installed skills, identifies redundancy between skills, and helps authors/consumers optimize context window usage. This is `bundlephobia` for the skill ecosystem.

**PRD:** `prds/04-context-budget.md`
**Priority:** Quadrant 1 — High Novelty (5/5), High Impact (4/5)
**Owner:** skillsafe (100%)

## Why This Is Novel
No tool in any package ecosystem measures token cost. Skills are unique because:
1. **Hard ceiling** — Context windows have fixed limits (100K-200K tokens)
2. **Direct cost** — Every token loaded costs money per API call
3. **Attention dilution** — Larger contexts degrade agent performance

## Key Deliverables

### CLI Interface
```bash
skillsafe budget                           # Measure all installed skills
skillsafe budget --skill ai-sdk-core       # Measure a specific skill
skillsafe budget --detailed                # Breakdown by section
skillsafe budget --format json             # CI-friendly output
skillsafe budget --max-tokens 50000        # Budget limit (fail if exceeded)
skillsafe budget --compare baseline.json   # Compare snapshots
skillsafe budget --include-deps            # Include transitive dependencies
```

### Architecture (follows extractor/checker/reporter pattern)
```
src/
  commands/budget.ts           # CLI command handler
  budget/
    index.ts                   # Orchestrator
    tokenizer.ts               # Token counting (js-tiktoken with cl100k_base)
    analyzer.ts                # Per-skill analysis (section breakdown)
    redundancy/
      ngram.ts                 # N-gram overlap detection (Jaccard similarity)
    cost.ts                    # Pricing model + estimation
    comparison.ts              # Snapshot comparison
    reporters/
      terminal.ts              # Formatted table output
      json.ts                  # Machine-readable
      markdown.ts              # For PRs/docs
```

### Token Counting
- Use `js-tiktoken` (NOT native `tiktoken`) with `cl100k_base` encoding
- Counts within 5% of actual model tokenization are acceptable
- Support per-section breakdown by parsing markdown headings

### Redundancy Detection (Phase 1: N-gram overlap)
- Tokenize each skill into 4-grams
- Compute Jaccard similarity between all skill pairs
- Flag pairs with > 0.2 similarity with consolidation suggestions

### Cost Estimation
- Maintain pricing table for common models (Claude Opus/Sonnet/Haiku, GPT-4o)
- Report cost as 'per 1,000 skill loads' at relevant price point

### CI Integration
- `--max-tokens N` exits with code 1 if total exceeds N
- `--format json` for machine-readable output
- `--save` / `--compare` for budget tracking over time

## Dependencies
- `js-tiktoken` (new dependency — JS port avoids platform-specific build issues)
- Existing skill discovery infrastructure
- Existing reporter patterns from audit command

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Token counts within 5% of actual model tokenization
- [ ] Redundancy detection identifies overlapping content with <10% false positive rate
- [ ] Budget measurement for 50 skills completes in <5 seconds
- [ ] Comparison mode clearly shows budget impact of skill changes
- [ ] All reporters (terminal, json, markdown) produce correct output
- [ ] `--max-tokens` flag works for CI gating
- [ ] Tests cover tokenizer, analyzer, redundancy, cost, comparison, and reporters
- [ ] No new deps beyond js-tiktoken
- [ ] README.md and CLAUDE.md updated","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"feature","owner":"voodootikigod@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-03T18:44:46Z","created_by":"Chris Williams","updated_at":"2026-03-03T18:44:46Z"}

{"id":"ss-0dh.2","title":"Implement `skillsafe verify` command — Semver for Knowledge","description":"## Overview
Implement the `skillsafe verify` command that analyzes content changes between skill versions and validates that the declared version bump matches the actual scope of change. This is `cargo semver-checks` adapted for prose and instructions rather than API surfaces.

**PRD:** `prds/05-semver-verification.md`
**Priority:** Quadrant 1 — High Novelty (4/5), High Impact (4/5)
**Owner:** skillsafe (100%)

## The Problem
Code has clear semantics for versioning (removing a public function = major). Skills have no equivalent. When `product-version` bumps from 6.0 to 7.0, does that mean the skill was rewritten, or did someone just update a version number? This matters for trust — if `skillsafe check` reports a skill is current but content is stale, the version is lying.

## Knowledge Versioning Vocabulary
- **Major:** Package renames, core API removal, primary workflow changes, import path changes
- **Minor:** New features/APIs added, new sections documented, scope expanded
- **Patch:** Typo fixes, version number updates in examples, URL updates, minor rewording

## Key Deliverables

### CLI Interface
```bash
skillsafe verify --skill ai-sdk-core                  # Verify single skill
skillsafe verify --all                                  # All skills against git history
skillsafe verify --before v1/ --after v2/               # Compare two versions
skillsafe verify --suggest --skill ai-sdk-core          # Auto-suggest version bump
skillsafe verify --format json                          # CI output
```

### Architecture
```
src/
  commands/verify.ts          # CLI command handler
  verify/
    index.ts                  # Orchestrator
    diff/
      structural.ts           # Section, code block, reference diffs
      content.ts              # Text similarity measurement
      packages.ts             # Package reference comparison (reuse audit extractors)
    classifier/
      heuristics.ts           # Rule-based classification (fast, deterministic)
      llm.ts                  # LLM-assisted classification (deeper, slower)
      combined.ts             # Combine heuristic + LLM results
    git.ts                    # Get previous version from git history
    reporters/
      terminal.ts
      json.ts
```

### Two-Layer Analysis

**Layer 1: Heuristic Rules (fast, free, deterministic)**
- Package rename detection → major signal (confidence 0.9)
- Deprecated/removed API patterns → major signal (confidence 0.7)
- New sections added → minor signal (confidence 0.8)
- Only version numbers changed → patch signal (confidence 0.8)
- Content similarity >95% → patch signal (confidence 0.7)

**Layer 2: LLM-Assisted (deeper, when heuristics uncertain)**
- Compare two versions via LLM prompt
- Classify as MAJOR/MINOR/PATCH with reasoning
- Only invoked when heuristic confidence <0.8
- Uses existing Vercel AI SDK infrastructure in src/llm/
- Must work without LLM key (graceful degradation to heuristics only)

### Combination Strategy
1. Run heuristic rules (fast, free)
2. If heuristics agree with high confidence (>0.8), use that result
3. If uncertain or disagreeing, run LLM analysis
4. Compare combined assessment against declared version bump
5. Report match or mismatch

## Integration with Pipeline
```bash
skillsafe check    # Find stale skills
skillsafe refresh  # AI-assisted update
skillsafe verify   # Validate the version bump ← THIS
skillsafe test     # Run regression tests
```

## Dependencies
- Existing audit extractors (packages.ts, commands.ts) for data extraction
- Existing LLM infrastructure (src/llm/) for AI-assisted classification
- Git CLI for version history
- No new external dependencies needed

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Correctly classifies package renames as major changes
- [ ] Correctly classifies new-section additions as minor changes
- [ ] Correctly classifies typo-only fixes as patch changes
- [ ] Heuristic-only mode runs in <2 seconds per skill
- [ ] LLM-assisted mode runs in <15 seconds per skill
- [ ] Works without LLM API key (heuristics-only fallback)
- [ ] Integrates with check → refresh pipeline
- [ ] Reuses audit extractors where applicable
- [ ] Tests cover heuristics, LLM classifier (mocked), git integration, reporters
- [ ] README.md and CLAUDE.md updated","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"feature","owner":"voodootikigod@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-03T18:45:13Z","created_by":"Chris Williams","updated_at":"2026-03-03T18:45:13Z"}

{"id":"ss-0dh.3","title":"Implement `skillsafe lint` command — Metadata Validation & Auto-fix","description":"## Overview
Implement the `skillsafe lint` command that validates metadata completeness in SKILL.md frontmatter, detects conditionally required fields, and offers auto-fix capabilities. This extends the metadata checker from `audit` into a standalone command with deeper validation and auto-fix.

**PRD:** `prds/10-mandatory-metadata.md`
**Priority:** Quadrant 2 — Lower Novelty (2/5), High Impact (4/5)
**Owner:** skillsafe (local lint side) — skills.sh owns publish-time enforcement
**Note:** This is the local developer experience side. skills.sh enforces on publish.

## Scope Boundary
- **skillsafe lint:** Local validation, auto-fix suggestions, CI gating
- **skills.sh publish:** Registry-side enforcement (not our concern)
- The audit command already has a basic metadata checker — lint extends it with deeper validation, conditional requirements, format checking, and auto-fix

## Key Deliverables

### CLI Interface
```bash
skillsafe lint                     # Lint all skills for metadata completeness
skillsafe lint --fix               # Auto-fix missing fields from context
skillsafe lint --ci --fail-on error  # CI mode with strict exit codes
skillsafe lint --format json       # Machine-readable output
```

### Architecture
```
src/
  commands/lint.ts              # CLI command handler
  lint/
    index.ts                    # Orchestrator
    rules/
      required.ts               # Always-required fields (name, description, author, license, repository)
      conditional.ts            # Conditionally required (product-version if references product, agents if agent-specific)
      recommended.ts            # Recommended fields (spec-version, keywords, min-context)
      format.ts                 # Field format validation (semver, URL, SPDX license)
    detection/
      product-refs.ts           # Detect product references in content
      agent-specific.ts         # Detect agent-specific instructions
    autofix.ts                  # Auto-fix missing fields from git config, remotes, etc.
    reporters/
      terminal.ts
      json.ts
```

### Validation Rules

**Always Required:**
- `name` — string, max 100 chars
- `description` — string, 20-500 chars

**Required for Publish (error):**
- `author` — string (who maintains this)
- `license` — valid SPDX identifier or expression
- `repository` — valid URL

**Conditionally Required:**
- `product-version` — required if skill references a specific product (semver format)
- `agents` — required if skill has agent-specific instructions (array of strings)

**Recommended (warning):**
- `spec-version` — which version of the agent skills spec
- `keywords` — discovery tags (array, at least 1 item)
- `min-context` — minimum context tokens needed

### Product Reference Detection Heuristics
- Mentions product name + version number (e.g., 'Next.js 15', 'AI SDK 6.x')
- Contains `npm install` / `pip install` commands for specific packages
- References product-specific APIs or configuration files

### Auto-fix Capabilities (`--fix`)
- Add `author` from `git config user.name`
- Add `repository` from `git remote get-url origin`
- Add template `license: "MIT"` with TODO comment
- Add `product-version` by detecting product references and querying npm for latest

### Relationship to Audit Metadata Checker
The existing `audit` command has `checkers/metadata.ts` which checks for basic required frontmatter (name, description, product-version). The `lint` command extends this:
- More fields validated (author, license, repository, agents, spec-version, keywords)
- Conditional requirement detection (not just 'is field present')
- Format validation (SPDX, semver, URL)
- Auto-fix capability
- The audit metadata checker should remain as a quick sanity check; lint is the thorough validation

## Dependencies
- Existing frontmatter parsing from audit pipeline
- Git CLI for auto-fix (git config, git remote)
- No new external dependencies (SPDX validation can use a simple list)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `lint` catches 95%+ of metadata issues before publish
- [ ] `--fix` successfully auto-fills author and repository from git context
- [ ] Product reference detection has <15% false positive rate
- [ ] SPDX license validation works for standard identifiers and expressions
- [ ] CI mode (`--ci --fail-on error`) exits non-zero on errors
- [ ] Does not duplicate audit's metadata checker — extends it
- [ ] Tests cover all rule types, detection heuristics, auto-fix, and reporters
- [ ] README.md and CLAUDE.md updated","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","owner":"voodootikigod@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-03T18:45:43Z","created_by":"Chris Williams","updated_at":"2026-03-03T18:45:43Z"}
